facts.text: Rocky Point grew from a rural settlement into a radio hub when RCA built a massive transmitting station there in the early 1900s.
The 450‑foot long‑wave towers once linked Europe and South America, making Rocky Point a key point‑to‑point communication site.
RCA’s Radio Central began transatlantic radio messages in 1921, and the first commercial telephone link between London and New York followed in 1927.
After the station closed in 1938, a replica now houses a Veterans of Foreign Wars museum on the original site.
The former Rocky Point railroad station building now serves as the main office of Thurber Lumber, after the tracks were removed.
In 2013 the Rocky Point Historical Society acquired the Noah Hallock Homestead, preserving local artifacts and archival photos for future generations.
